Read /home/codexssh/phys3/sbootstrap_v4d.tex and look for the following specific issues:

1. Any remaining instances where the bion Kähler term uses M^k_k (meson fields) instead of \hat m_k (quark masses) — besides the one at line 2772 which correctly explains the map between them.

2. Check the "Precision summary" section (around line 382) and verify the numbers in the precision table are internally consistent with what's stated elsewhere in the paper (especially the ppm figures, sigma values).

3. Look at the Yukawa terms in the unified superpotential (around line 2670): $y_c H M^c_c + y_b H M^b_b + y_t H Q^t \bar{Q}_t$. Check whether the paper explains the distinction between the effective coupling $H M^c_c$ (coupling to composite meson) vs the fundamental coupling $H Q^t \bar{Q}_t$ (coupling to elementary quarks).

4. Check if there are any other formula/notation inconsistencies you can spot.

Report your findings. Do NOT make any edits, just report.

content.text: I'll read the file in sections to thoroughly check all the specified issues.

1→\documentclass[12pt,a4paper]{article}
     2→\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
     3→\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
     4→\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsfonts}
     5→\usepackage{mathtools}
     6→\usepackage{booktabs}
     7→\usepackage{array}
     8→\usepackage{geometry}
     9→\usepackage{hyperref}
    10→\usepackage{cite}
    11→\geometry{margin=2.5cm}
    12→
    13→\newcommand{\MeV}{\,\text{MeV}}
    14→\newcommand{\GeV}{\,\text{GeV}}
    15→\newcommand{\fpi}{f_\pi}
    16→\newcommand{\qqbar}{\langle\bar{q}q\rangle}
    17→\newcommand{\zch}{\mathfrak{z}}
    18→
    19→\title{A Tale of Two Tuples: Seed Triples, Signed Charges, \\
    20→and SUSY Breaking in the sBootstrap}
    21→
    22→\author{A.~Rivero\thanks{Manuscript prepared with AI assistance (Claude/Anthropic). 
    23→Continues the program of \cite{Rivero2005,Rivero2012}.}}
    24→\date{March 2026}
    25→
    26→\begin{document}
    27→\maketitle
    28→
    29→\begin{abstract}
    30→We reformulate the sBootstrap in terms of signed mass charges 
    31→$\zch = z_0 + z_i$ with $m = \zch^2$, and show that the Koide ratio 
    32→$Q = 2/3$ is the algebraic consequence of two conditions: 
    33→$\sum z_i = 0$ and $\langle z_i^2\rangle = z_0^2$ (perturbation 
    34→energies average to the base-scale energy). Allowing negative charges, 
    35→we find that pseudoscalar mesons satisfy a Koide triple 
    36→$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ to 0.1\%, and identify \emph{seed triples} with 
    37→one massless member---$(0, s, c)$ for quarks, $(0, \pi, D_s)$ for 
    38→mesons---as the pre-breaking configurations from which the full 
    39→spectrum emerges. These seed triples share the same analytical 
    40→structure (charge ratio $2-\sqrt{3}$) and, strikingly, the meson 
    41→seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ has Koide scale $v_0^2 \approx 351\MeV$,
    42→close to the lepton triple's $v_0^2 \approx 314\MeV$. A third 
    43→tuple $(c,b,t)$ stands apart, having no meson counterpart because 
    44→top diquarks do not hadronize. We discuss the diquark sector---which 
    45→fills the symmetric $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ of $SU(5)$ 
    46→flavor, in conflict with Pauli statistics for point-like diquarks, 
    47→proving that the diquark cannot be a $qq$ composite---and outline 
    48→how SUSY breaking proceeds through two mechanisms---F-term (chiral
    49→condensate, with Volkov--Akulov as its low-energy realization) and
    50→D-term (electromagnetic Fayet--Iliopoulos)---plus a separate
    51→heavy-sector origin for the $(c,b,t)$ triple. The O'Raifeartaigh
    52→superpotential produces the Koide seed spectrum exactly when
    53→$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$, reducing the energy-balance condition to a
    54→single constraint on the couplings. The bloom from seed to full
    55→triple doubles the vacuum charge, yielding
    56→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ (matching PDG to
    57→$0.1\sigma$), and all quark Koide triples obligatorily mix
    58→up-type and down-type mass eigenstates.
    59→We construct the explicit $\mathcal{N}=1$ Lagrangian---K\"ahler
    60→potential, superpotential (Seiberg constraint plus Yukawa),
    61→$D$-terms, and soft breaking from a spurion with
    62→$\langle F\rangle = f_\pi^2$---and show that the seed Koide
    63→fixes the magnetic Yukawa coupling ($h = \sqrt{3}$) while the
    64→$v_0$-doubling originates in the K\"ahler sector via a bion
    65→correction $|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$ from
    66→monopole-instantons on $\mathbb{R}^3\times S^1$; a
    67→three-instanton superpotential $(\det M)^3/\Lambda^{18}$
    68→generates a $\cos(9\delta)$ potential whose minima include the
    69→observed lepton Koide phase to $5\,\text{ppm}$.
    70→After these constraints, only two to three
    71→free parameters remain ($m_u$, $m_d$, $m_s$), with $m_u$ and
    72→$m_d$ connected to the Cabibbo angle via the Oakes relation.
    73→The $SO(32)$ adjoint accommodates the sBootstrap representations;
    74→$E_8 \times E_8$ is blocked because $E_8$ decompositions produce
    75→only antisymmetric $\mathbf{10}$, never the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$
    76→required for diquarks. A Diophantine system on
    77→the scalar content uniquely selects $N=3$ generations.
    78→\end{abstract}
    79→
    80→\tableofcontents
    81→
    82→%======================================================================
    83→\section{Introduction}
    84→\label{sec:intro}
    85→%======================================================================
    86→
    87→The sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005,Rivero2012} proposes that the Standard 
    88→Model's pseudoscalar mesons and leptons fill common $\mathcal{N}=1$ 
    89→supermultiplets. Its foundational relation is
    90→\begin{equation}
    91→\label{eq:pimu}
    92→m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2\,,
    93→\end{equation}
    94→identifying the pion decay constant $f_\pi \approx 92.2\MeV$ as the 
    95→SUSY-breaking order parameter. Numerically, $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = 
    96→8{,}316\MeV^2$ versus $f_\pi^2 = 8{,}501\MeV^2$, a $2.2\%$ 
    97→discrepancy---comparable to the precision of the quark Koide triples 
    98→discussed below. In this paper we develop two themes 
    99→that change the structure of the program.
   100→

340→$b$ quark and $B$ meson are always present as flavor degrees of 
   341→freedom; what the breaking does is give them their \emph{distinctive 
   342→masses}---the specific values that complete the Koide triple. Before 
   343→breaking, the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction ($\zch = 0$); 
   344→after, it acquires $\zch_b = 64.65\MeV^{1/2}$, the value selected 
   345→by the energy balance with $c$ and the sign-flipped $s$. The bloom 
   346→is not particle creation but \emph{mass generation}.
   347→\end{itemize}
   348→
   349→The ``tale of two tuples'' is this: \textbf{each sector presents
   350→first as a seed with a massless member, then ``blooms'' into a
   351→full triple when SUSY breaks.} The massless member is the necessary
   352→initial condition; the breaking lifts it and generates the full spectrum.
   353→
   354→\paragraph{The vacuum-charge doubling (new).}
   355→The vacuum charge $v_0 = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum\zch_k$ of the quark seed
   356→is $v_0^{(s)} = (\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c})/3 = 15.10\MeV^{1/2}$.
   357→For the full triple, $v_0^{(f)} = (-\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} +
   358→\sqrt{m_b})/3 = 30.21\MeV^{1/2}$. Their ratio is
   359→\begin{equation*}
   360→  v_0^{(f)}/v_0^{(s)} = 2.0005 \approx 2\,.
   361→\end{equation*}
   362→If the bloom exactly doubles the vacuum charge, then
   363→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$, giving
   364→$m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$---matching the PDG value
   365→$4{,}180 \pm 30\MeV$ to $0.1\sigma$. This relation is
   366→independent of the Koide condition: it constrains $m_b$ from
   367→$(m_s, m_c)$ alone, and is more precise (0.07\%) than the
   368→overlapping-triples prediction (0.5\%).  A tension exists, however:
   369→the exact seed Koide gives $m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$ (not the PDG
   370→$1{,}270$), and applying the $v_0$-doubling to this exact seed
   371→value gives $m_b = 4{,}233\MeV$ ($1.8\sigma$ from PDG). The
   372→$v_0$-doubling works \emph{with the physical} $m_c$, not the exact
   373→seed value---so these two constraints are mutually inconsistent at the
   374→stated precision. The resolution may be that the seed Koide fixes the
   375→ratio $m_c/m_s$ only approximately (the 2.4\% charm deviation being a
   376→higher-order correction to the $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ condition), while the
   377→$v_0$-doubling constrains $m_b$ directly from the physical masses. In
   378→this reading, the seed condition is the approximate one and the
   379→$v_0$-doubling the more fundamental. This hierarchy is not derived;
   380→it is imposed by the data.
   381→
   382→\subsection{Precision summary}
   383→
   384→\begin{center}
   385→\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
   386→\toprule
   387→Triple & $Q$ & Dev.\ from $2/3$ & $v_0^2$ (MeV) & $\delta$ (rad) \\
   388→\midrule
   389→\multicolumn{5}{c}{\textit{Seed triples}} \\
   390→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.6679 & 0.18\% & 351 & $\approx 3\pi/4$ \\
   391→$(0, s, c)$ & 0.6644 & 0.35\% & 228 & $\approx 3\pi/4$ \\
   392→$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.6667 & 0.001\% & 314 & 2.317 \\
   393→\midrule
   394→\multicolumn{5}{c}{\textit{Full triples}} \\
   395→$(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.66666 & 0.001\% & 314 & 2.317 \\
   396→$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ & 0.6674 & 0.10\% & 1230 & 2.806 \\
   397→$(-s, c, b)$ & 0.6750 & 1.24\% & 913 & 2.744 \\
   398→$(c, b, t)$ & 0.6695 & 0.42\% & 29\,580 & 2.163 \\
   399→\bottomrule
   400→\end{tabular}
   401→\end{center}
   402→
   403→The Koide phases $\delta$ cluster in the range $2.2$--$2.8$~rad
   404→($126^\circ$--$161^\circ$), all displaced from the seed value
   405→$3\pi/4 \approx 2.356$. The lepton triple barely moved from its
   406→seed; the quark and meson triples bloomed further. All phases
   407→lie within one $\mathbb{Z}_3$ fundamental domain ($[0, 2\pi/3]$
   408→modulo $2\pi/3$).
   409→
   410→The lepton triple is unique: seed and full triple are nearly
   411→identical, because the electron mass is small enough to serve as
   412→the ``zero'' while being nonzero enough to make $Q$ extremely
   413→precise. The $(-s,c,b)$ triple has the worst deviation (1.24\%);
   414→this is expected because the bloom is a nonperturbative process
   415→that does not exactly preserve $Q$. The $v_0$-doubling constrains
   416→$m_b$ independently (to 0.07\% via $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} +
   417→\sqrt{m_c}$), so the 1.24\% Koide deviation is the residual after
   418→the more fundamental $v_0$-constraint is satisfied.
   419→
   420→\subsection{Orthogonality of seed and full triples}
   421→
   422→Each Koide triple defines a perturbation vector 
   423→$\vec{z} = (z_1, z_2, z_3)$ constrained to the plane $\sum z_k = 0$ 
   424→with length $|\vec{z}|^2 = 3z_0^2$. The angle between seed and 
   425→full triples measures how much the breaking rotates the spectrum 
   426→in charge space:
   427→
   428→\begin{center}
   429→\begin{tabular}{lc}
   430→\toprule
   431→Pair of triples & Angle \\
   432→\midrule
   433→$(0,s,c)$ vs.\ $(-s,c,b)$ & $22^\circ$ \\
   434→$(0,\pi,D_s)$ vs.\ $(-\pi,D_s,B)$ & $26^\circ$ \\
   435→$(e,\mu,\tau)$ vs.\ $(0,\mu,\tau)$ & $0.8^\circ$ \\
   436→$(0,s,c)$ vs.\ $(0,\pi,D_s)$ & $0.3^\circ$ \\
   437→\bottomrule
   438→\end{tabular}
   439→\end{center}
   440→
   441→Two results are notable. First, the quark seed $(0,s,c)$ and meson 
   442→seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ are \textbf{nearly parallel} ($0.3^\circ$): the 
   443→meson seed is the composite image of the quark seed, with the GOR 
   444→map preserving direction while changing scale. Second, the lepton 
   445→triple is essentially its own seed ($0.8^\circ$)---the bloom has 
   446→barely begun.
   447→
   448→
   449→%======================================================================
   450→\section{The Meson Koide Triple: $(-\pi, D_s, B)$}
   451→\label{sec:mesonkoide}
   452→%======================================================================
   453→
   454→With the pion charge negative, the triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ satisfies:
   455→\begin{equation}
   456→Q(-\pi, D_s, B) = 
   457→\frac{m_\pi + m_{D_s} + m_B}{(-\sqrt{m_\pi} + \sqrt{m_{D_s}} + \sqrt{m_B})^2}
   458→= 0.6674\,,
   459→\end{equation}

2640→%
  2641→\begin{equation*}
  2642→  \mathcal{L}
  2643→  = \int\!d^4\theta\,K
  2644→  + \left[\int\!d^2\theta\,W + \text{h.c.}\right]
  2645→  + \mathcal{L}_D
  2646→  + \mathcal{L}_{\text{soft}}\,,
  2647→\end{equation*}
  2648→%
  2649→with K\"ahler potential (canonical plus bion correction),
  2650→%
  2651→\begin{equation*}
  2652→  K = \underbrace{\operatorname{Tr}(M^\dagger M) + |X|^2 + |B|^2
  2653→    + |\bar B|^2 + |H|^2}_{K_{\text{can}}}
  2654→  + \underbrace{\frac{\zeta^2}{\Lambda^2}\,
  2655→    \Bigl|\sum_{k=1}^{N_c} s_k \sqrt{\hat m_k}\,\Bigr|^2}_{K_{\text{bion}}}\,,
  2656→\end{equation*}
  2657→where $\zeta \propto e^{-S_0/(2N_c)}$ is the monopole fugacity,
  2658→$\hat m_k$ are the current quark masses (the couplings in
  2659→$W \ni \operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\,M)$), and
  2660→$s_k = \pm 1$ are monopole-instanton signs (encoding the seed-to-bloom
  2661→phase). The bion term is non-holomorphic (containing $\sqrt{\hat m}$) and
  2662→non-perturbatively suppressed by $e^{-2S_0/3} \sim 10^{-2}$ at the
  2663→confinement scale. Its minimum enforces the $v_0$-doubling condition
  2664→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$
  2665→(\S\ref{subsec:fterm});
  2666→%
  2667→superpotential combining mass, constraint, and Yukawa terms,
  2668→%
  2669→\begin{equation*}
  2670→  W = \underbrace{\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)}_{\text{chiral mass}}
  2671→    + \underbrace{X\!\left(\det M^{(3)} - B\bar B
  2672→      - \Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{6}\right)}_{\text{Seiberg constraint}}
  2673→    + \underbrace{c_3\,\frac{(\det M^{(3)})^3}
  2674→      {\Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{18}}}_{\text{3-instanton}}
  2675→    + \underbrace{y_c\, H\, M^c{}_c
  2676→      + y_b\, H\, M^b{}_b
  2677→      + y_t\, H\, Q^t \bar Q_t\phantom{X}}_{\text{Yukawa}}\,,
  2678→\end{equation*}
  2679→%
  2680→where $M^{(3)}$ is the $3\times 3$ light-flavor block ($u,d,s$)
  2681→and $\Lambda_{\text{eff}}$ is the dynamical scale of the confined
  2682→$N_f = N_c = 3$ theory (including threshold matching at $m_c$
  2683→and $m_b$).
  2684→The $D$-term Lagrangian includes an optional Fayet--Iliopoulos
  2685→parameter $\xi$ for $U(1)_{\text{em}}$:
  2686→%
  2687→\begin{equation*}
  2688→  \mathcal{L}_D
  2689→  = -\frac{e^2}{2}\!\left(\sum_i Q_i\,|\phi_i|^2
  2690→    + \xi\right)^{\!2}.
  2691→\end{equation*}
  2692→%
  2693→Finally, soft SUSY-breaking terms arise from a spurion
  2694→$\langle F_S\rangle = \fpi^2$:
  2695→%
  2696→\begin{equation*}
  2697→  \mathcal{L}_{\text{soft}}
  2698→  = -\tilde m^2 \operatorname{Tr}(M^\dagger M)
  2699→  - \bigl(B_\mu\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)
  2700→    + A_y\, y_i\, H\, M^i{}_i + \text{h.c.}\bigr)\,,
  2701→  \qquad
  2702→  \tilde m^2 \sim \fpi^2\,.
  2703→\end{equation*}
  2704→%
  2705→The soft scalar mass $\tilde m^2 \sim \fpi^2 \approx (92\MeV)^2$
  2706→generates the universal meson--lepton splitting
  2707→$m^2_{\text{meson}} - m^2_{\text{lepton}} = \fpi^2$.
  2708→
  2709→\paragraph{Scope of the Lagrangian.}
  2710→This Lagrangian produces: (i)~the seed Koide condition ($gv/m = \sqrt{3}$
  2711→in the O'Raifeartaigh limit); (ii)~the meson--lepton mass splitting
  2712→($\fpi^2$); (iii)~the $v_0$-doubling condition via the bion K\"ahler
  2713→correction $K_{\text{bion}}$, whose minimum enforces
  2714→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$; (iv)~the Yukawa couplings for
  2715→the heavy sector; (v)~the $\cos(9\delta)$ potential that selects
  2716→the lepton Koide phase, via the three-instanton operator
  2717→$(\det M^{(3)})^3/\Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{18}$. The bloom is
  2718→non-holomorphic and lives in the K\"ahler sector: the bion term
  2719→$|\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$ is the leading monopole-instanton
  2720→contribution on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$
  2721→(\S\ref{subsec:fterm}).
  2722→
  2723→\paragraph{Mass spectrum from the Lagrangian.}
  2724→The mass of each particle is determined by a specific term:
  2725→\begin{center}
  2726→\small
  2727→\begin{tabular}{lll}
  2728→\toprule
  2729→Mass & Origin & Equation \\
  2730→\midrule
  2731→$m_{\text{lepton}}^2 = |\hat m_i + \hat m_j|^2$
  2732→  & $W$: fermion mass from $\partial^2 W/\partial M_i \partial M_j$ & $F$-term \\
  2733→$m_{\text{meson}}^2 = m_{\text{lepton}}^2 + \fpi^2$
  2734→  & $\mathcal{L}_{\text{soft}}$: $\tilde m^2 \sim \fpi^2$ & Spurion \\
  2735→$m_c/m_s = (2+\sqrt{3})^2$
  2736→  & $W$: O'Raifeartaigh at $h = \sqrt{3}$ & Seed Koide \\
  2737→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$
  2738→  & $K_{\text{bion}}$: minimum of $|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2S_{\text{seed}}|^2$
  2739→  & $v_0$-doubling \\
  2740→$m_t = y_t v_{\text{EW}}/\sqrt{2}$
  2741→  & $W_{\text{Yukawa}}$: $y_t H Q^t\bar Q_t$ & External \\
  2742→$\delta m^2 = Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi$
  2743→  & $\mathcal{L}_D$: FI term & EM splitting \\
  2744→$\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$
  2745→  & $W$: $(\det M^{(3)})^3/\Lambda^{18}$ & 3-instanton \\
  2746→\bottomrule
  2747→\end{tabular}
  2748→\end{center}
  2749→The first four rows account for six quark masses ($m_u$ and $m_d$ free,
  2750→$m_s$ sets the scale, $m_c$ from seed, $m_b$ from K\"ahler, $m_t$
  2751→external). The meson--lepton splitting is universal across the
  2752→multiplet, and the $D$-term provides the subdominant electromagnetic
  2753→correction.
  2754→
  2755→The parameters of this construction separate into those fixed by
  2756→structure and those that remain free.
  2757→
  2758→\paragraph{Fixed by the Koide seed.}
  2759→The O'Raifeartaigh condition $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ translates, in the
  2760→ISS magnetic dual, into a fixed magnetic Yukawa coupling $h = \sqrt{3}$.
  2761→This fixes the mass ratio $m_c/m_s = (2+\sqrt{3})^2$
  2762→within the seed triple, predicting $m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$ from $m_s$ alone
  2763→(PDG: $1{,}270\MeV$, 2.4\% deviation).
  2764→
  2765→\paragraph{Fixed by the vacuum-charge doubling.}
  2766→The bloom from seed to full triple doubles $v_0$, giving
  2767→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ and predicting
  2768→$m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$ (0.1$\sigma$ from PDG). This constraint
  2769→is \emph{non-holomorphic}: it is a linear relation among square
  2770→roots of masses, while the superpotential is holomorphic in meson
  2771→fields whose VEVs scale as $1/m_i$. The map
  2772→$\zch_k = \sqrt{m_k} \sim 1/\!\sqrt{\langle M^k{}_k\rangle}$
  2773→is non-holomorphic. The $v_0$-doubling must therefore arise from the
  2774→K\"ahler potential, nonperturbative effects, or emergent dynamics---it
  2775→is not a tree-level superpotential term.
  2776→
  2777→\paragraph{Fixed by SUSY breaking.}
  2778→The supertrace relation $m^2_\pi - m^2_\mu = \fpi^2$ fixes the
  2779→splitting scale. The identification $\langle F\rangle \sim \fpi$
  2780→links $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ to $m_s$ via the GOR relation.
  2781→
  2782→\paragraph{$D$-term effects on the Koide condition.}
  2783→In $\mathcal{N}=1$ SUSY, $D$-terms shift scalar masses but leave
  2784→fermion masses untouched. The lepton Koide triples, being conditions
  2785→on \emph{fermion} masses, are naturally protected against
  2786→$D$-term perturbations. The meson Koide triple
  2787→$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ involves scalar masses and \emph{is} in principle
  2788→affected by $D$-terms: a FI parameter $\xi$ shifts $m^2 \to m^2 + \xi Q_{\text{em}}^2$.
  2789→Since all three mesons carry unit charge, each $m^2$ shifts by the
  2790→same $\xi$, and the relative correction to $Q$ is of order
  2791→$\xi/(4m_\pi^2) \sim 0.1\%$ for $\xi \approx -80\MeV^2$.
  2792→Numerically, the $D$-term shifts the meson $Q$ by $0.03\%$,
  2793→well below the $0.10\%$ precision of the triple.
  2794→
  2795→\paragraph{Remaining free parameters.}
  2796→After the seed Koide fixes $m_c/m_s$, the $v_0$-doubling fixes
  2797→$m_b$, and $h=\sqrt{3}$ is determined, only two to three free
  2798→parameters remain:
  2799→\begin{itemize}
  2800→  \item $m_u$, $m_d$: unconstrained by any Koide triple; connected
  2801→    to the Cabibbo angle through the Oakes relation
  2802→    $\tan\theta_C \approx \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ (giving $V_{us} = 0.224$,
  2803→    PDG: $0.2243$);
  2804→  \item $m_s$ (or equivalently $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$): the overall
  2805→    scale of the light sector.
  2806→\end{itemize}
  2807→The top mass $m_t$ is external---it does not participate in the
  2808→confined meson matrix. The Yukawa couplings $y_c = \sqrt{2}\,m_c/v_{\text{EW}}$,
  2809→$y_b = \sqrt{2}\,m_b/v_{\text{EW}}$ are fixed by the predicted masses.
  2810→
  2811→The $(c,b,t)$ Koide triple provides an \emph{overdetermination test}:
  2812→with $m_c$ and $m_b$ already predicted and $m_t$ external,
  2813→$Q(c,b,t) = 0.670$ should be close to $2/3$---and it is
  2814→(0.42\% deviation). If both overlapping triples are required to
  2815→satisfy $Q = 2/3$ exactly, the overlap system determines
  2816→$m_b^{\text{pred}} = 4{,}159\MeV$ (0.5\% from PDG) and

1820→carries a single fermionic zero mode per flavor; when the zero mode is
  1821→lifted by the quark mass $m_i$, the amplitude acquires a factor
  1822→$\sqrt{m_i}$ (not $m_i$). Since the $N_c = 3$ monopole contributions
  1823→enter \emph{additively} in the effective superpotential,
  1824→\begin{equation*}
  1825→  W_{\text{mon}} \sim \zeta\,\bigl(\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2} + \sqrt{m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1826→\end{equation*}
  1827→where $\zeta \propto e^{-S_0/3}$ is the monopole fugacity. This is
  1828→\emph{exactly} the sum-of-square-roots structure of the vacuum charge
  1829→$v_0 = (\sum \sqrt{m_k})/3$.
  1830→The $v_0$-doubling then maps to the counting of active
  1831→monopole-instanton contributions: the seed (two active flavors) involves
  1832→two monopole amplitudes, while the bloom (three active flavors, one with
  1833→a sign flip) involves three, doubling $v_0$.
  1834→
  1835→This mechanism is non-holomorphic in the sense required: the
  1836→K\"ahler potential receives monopole--anti-monopole (bion) cross terms
  1837→of the form $|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$, where $m_k$ are the
  1838→current quark masses. Expanding for $N_c = 3$:
  1839→\begin{equation*}
  1840→  K_{\text{bion}} = \frac{\zeta^2}{\Lambda^2}\bigl(
  1841→  m_1 + m_2 + m_3
  1842→  + 2s_1 s_2\sqrt{m_1 m_2}
  1843→  + 2s_1 s_3\sqrt{m_1 m_3}
  1844→  + 2s_2 s_3\sqrt{m_2 m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1845→\end{equation*}
  1846→where the diagonal terms $s_k^2 m_k = m_k$ are self-bions and the
  1847→cross terms $\sqrt{m_i m_j}$ are mixed bion amplitudes---their
  1848→geometric mean structure is what produces $\sum\sqrt{m_k}$.
  1849→Note that $m_k$ here are the quark masses (superpotential couplings),
  1850→\emph{not} the meson VEVs $M^k{}_k \propto 1/m_k$ of the Seiberg
  1851→seesaw; the bion amplitude involves quark zero modes, not meson fields.
  1852→Organizing the bion amplitudes into
  1853→seed-sector ($S_{\text{seed}} = \sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$) and
  1854→bloom-sector ($S_{\text{bloom}} = -\!\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} + \sqrt{m_b}$)
  1855→channels, the K\"ahler correction takes the form
  1856→\begin{equation*}
  1857→  \delta K \sim \lambda_1\,|S_{\text{seed}}|^2
  1858→  + \lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2\,.
  1859→\end{equation*}
  1860→The second term is a perfect square that vanishes
  1861→\emph{at the minimum} when $S_{\text{bloom}} = 2\,S_{\text{seed}}$,
  1862→i.e., $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$---the
  1863→$v_0$-doubling condition. The relation between bion couplings
  1864→($\lambda_3 = -4\lambda_2$ for cross-channel bions) is what
  1865→enforces the doubling. Numerically,
  1866→$S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}} = 0.023\MeV^{1/2}$
  1867→(effectively zero: $m_b = 4{,}177$ vs.\ PDG $4{,}180$).
  1868→The Hessian of $\lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2$
  1869→has rank~1: its single nonzero eigenvector is
  1870→$\partial f/\partial m_i$ (the gradient of
  1871→$f = \sqrt{m_b} - 3\sqrt{m_s} - \sqrt{m_c}$), while the two flat
  1872→directions correspond to independent variation of $m_s$ and $m_c$
  1873→at fixed $f$.  Thus the bion K\"ahler constrains exactly \emph{one}
  1874→mass combination; the remaining two masses are fixed by the Koide
  1875→seed in the superpotential. Including propagated uncertainties
  1876→($\delta m_s = 0.8\MeV$, $\delta m_c = 20\MeV$), the prediction
  1877→is $m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$, consistent with the PDG value
  1878→$4{,}180 \pm 30\MeV$ at $0.06\sigma$.
  1879→
  1880→The principal caveat is that this analysis
  1881→applies on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$; connecting to $\mathbb{R}^4$
  1882→physics requires \"Unsal's continuity conjecture~\cite{Unsal2012},
  1883→which posits that center-symmetric confinement at small $S^1$ is
  1884→continuously connected to the large-$S^1$ confining vacuum.
  1885→The conjecture is supported by lattice evidence for $SU(N)$ pure
  1886→Yang--Mills and by the absence of phase transitions in the
  1887→center-stabilized theory, but remains unproven for theories with
  1888→matter.
  1889→
  1890→The identification $f = \langle F_{\Phi_0}\rangle \sim f_\pi$ is the
  1891→sBootstrap's central claim: the SUSY-breaking $F$-term is the chiral
  1892→condensate parameter.
  1893→
  1894→In SQCD, this is realized dynamically rather than through a 
  1895→superpotential: the chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ acts as the 
  1896→nonzero $F$-term, the impossibility of restoring chiral symmetry 
  1897→in the QCD vacuum is the dynamical analogue of $f \neq 0$, and the 
  1898→pion---the would-be Goldstone boson that acquires mass from explicit 
  1899→chiral breaking---plays the role of $\Phi_0$ whose mass is lifted

  2574→  m^2_{\text{meson}} - m^2_{\text{lepton}} = f^2 \approx \fpi^2\,,

  2579→The top quark, with $m_t\gg\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, does not hadronize and

  2581→$N_f=5$ is exact. Its mass, and corrections to the heavy-quark sector
  2582→($c$, $b$), originate instead from electroweak symmetry breaking. Introducing
  2583→a Higgs doublet $H$, one writes a Yukawa superpotential
  2584→%
  2585→\begin{equation*}
  2586→  W_{\text{Yukawa}} = y_{ij}\, H\, Q^i\, \bar Q_j\,,
  2587→\end{equation*}

  2592→$\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)$. Note that $\hat m$ in the chiral
  2593→mass term already \emph{is} the current quark mass generated by the

  2595→superpotential is written separately to make explicit the dependence
  2596→on the Higgs field $H$, which is needed for the heavy sector where

  2598→chiral symmetry breaking.

  2600→The Koide condition on the $(c,b,t)$ triple constrains the diagonal
  2601→Yukawa matrix $Y = \operatorname{diag}(y_c,y_b,y_t)$ with
  2602→$y_i = \sqrt{2}\,m_i/v_{\text{EW}}$. Since $Q$ is scale-invariant,
  2603→the Koide relation reads
  2604→%
  2605→\begin{equation*}
  2606→  \frac{y_c + y_b + y_t}
  2607→       {(\sqrt{y_c}+\sqrt{y_b}+\sqrt{y_t})^2}
  2608→  = \frac{2}{3}\,,
  2609→\end{equation*}
  2610→%
  2611→which is equivalent to writing the square roots of the Yukawa
  2612→couplings as
  2613→%
  2614→\begin{equation*}
  2615→  \sqrt{y_k} = \sqrt{y_0}\,
  2616→  \bigl(1 + \sqrt{2}\,\cos(2\pi k/3 + \delta)\bigr)\,,
  2617→  \qquad k = 0,1,2\,,
  2618→\end{equation*}
  2619→%
  2620→where $y_0 = \bigl(\sum\sqrt{y_k}\bigr)^2\!/9$ and $\delta$ is
  2621→the Koide phase. For the physical $(c,b,t)$ masses,
  2622→$\delta \approx 0.069$ and $Q = 0.6695$ (a 0.42\% deviation from
  2623→$2/3$). The traceless part of $\sqrt{Y}$ lies in the Cartan
  2624→subalgebra of $SU(3)_f$:
  2625→%
  2626→\begin{equation*}
  2627→  \sqrt{Y} = \sqrt{y_0}\,
  2628→  \Bigl(I_3 + \tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{2}\,
  2629→  \hat n\cdot\boldsymbol\lambda_{\text{diag}}\Bigr)\,,
  2630→\end{equation*}
  2631→%
  2632→where $\boldsymbol\lambda_{\text{diag}} = (\lambda_3, \lambda_8)$
  2633→are the diagonal Gell-Mann matrices and $\hat n$ is a unit vector
  2634→in the Cartan plane determined by $\delta$. The Koide condition is
  2635→therefore a constraint on the \emph{direction} of the Yukawa
  2636→matrix in flavor space: the amplitude $\sqrt{6}/2$ is fixed, only
  2637→$\hat n$ (equivalently $\delta$) is free.
  2638→

   655→\subsection{The scaling rule: $M_1 = 3M_0$, $\delta_1 = 3\delta_0$}
   656→\label{subsec:scaling}
   657→
   658→A natural question is whether other Koide triples can be obtained
   659→from the lepton parameters by a simple scaling. The ansatz
   660→\begin{equation}
   661→M_1 = 3\,M_0 = 941.5\MeV\,, \qquad
   662→\delta_1 = 3\,\delta_0 = 6.9497\;\text{rad}\,,
   663→\end{equation}
   664→inserted into~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}, predicts a quark triple.
   665→Table~\ref{tab:scaling_pred} compares predictions to PDG values.

   668→\centering

   670→central values. The quark Koide triple uses a negative sign for
   671→$\sqrt{m_s}$, as in \S\ref{sec:two_tuples}.}
   672→\label{tab:scaling_pred}

2920→The O'Raifeartaigh realization (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}) fixes the
  2921→seed ratio $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ but leaves $\delta$ undetermined---the
  2922→phase controls how the seed blooms into a full triple. The bloom
  2923→is a phase rotation $\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ at
  2924→fixed $v_0$, but explicit R-breaking perturbations push $Q$ away
  2925→from $2/3$ at any nonzero strength, making the seed an unstable
  2926→fixed point. The bloom must therefore be nonperturbative.
  2927→
  2928→The lepton phase satisfies $\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$ to
  2929→$5\,\text{ppm}$ ($3.6\sigma$ after look-elsewhere over fractions
  2930→with denominator $\leq 20$). Since $2/9$
  2931→is not a rational multiple of $\pi$, this is not a geometric angle.
  2932→It is, however, $2/9 = 2/(3\times 3)$, which points to a $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  2933→discrete symmetry in the Koide modulus space. The $\mathbb{Z}_3$
  2934→mass-permutation symmetry ($\delta \to \delta + 2\pi/3$) is the
  2935→unique subgroup; the quotient $\mathbb{Z}_9/\mathbb{Z}_3$ has three
  2936→cosets, producing three inequivalent mass spectra from the same $M_0$.
  2937→The simplest potential with $\mathbb{Z}_9$ symmetry,
  2938→$V \supset -\cos\bigl(9(\delta - 2/9)\bigr)$, has the lepton value
  2939→as one of its nine minima, with the $\mathbb{Z}_3$-equivalent minima
  2940→at $\delta = 2/9 + 2k\pi/3$.
  2941→
  2942→A dynamical origin for $\mathbb{Z}_9$ exists in SQCD: for $N_f = N_c = 3$,
  2943→the diagonal $U(1)_{R+2A}$ (R-symmetry plus twice the axial symmetry)
  2944→has anomaly coefficient $6N_c = 18 = 2 \times 9$. Instantons break
  2945→$U(1)_{R+2A} \to \mathbb{Z}_{18}$, which contains $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  2946→as a subgroup. The meson field $M$ carries $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ charge 4

There's an internal inconsistency on the $v_0$-doubling precision: the summary table at line 3133 says "to $0.025\%$" but the body text consistently says "0.07%" (lines 367, 416, 2818). Let me verify: $|4177 - 4180|/4180 = 3/4180 \approx 0.072\%$, so "0.07%" is correct. Where does "0.025%" come from?

Let me check whether "0.025%" might come from the $v_0$ ratio itself rather than $m_b$.

$v_0^{(f)}/v_0^{(s)} = 2.0005$ (from line 360). Deviation from 2 is $0.0005/2 = 0.025\%$. So the summary table measures the precision of the doubling ratio, while the body text measures the precision of the $m_b$ prediction. These are different quantities — and the table label "observed to $0.025\%$" is for the vacuum-charge doubling ratio ($v_0^{(f)}/v_0^{(s)} = 2.0005$), while the body text's "0.07%" refers to the resulting $m_b$ prediction. This is not actually inconsistent but it is very confusingly presented — two different ways of measuring the same coincidence give different percentages, and the table uses the tighter-looking one (0.025%) while the body uses the less tight one (0.07%).

Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile all findings.

Here is a complete report of what I found in `/home/codexssh/phys3/sbootstrap_v4d.tex`:

---

## Issue 1: Kähler term $M^k_k$ vs $\hat m_k$

There are NO remaining erroneous uses of $M^k{}_k$ in the Kähler sector. Every appearance of the bion Kähler term correctly uses $\hat m_k$ (quark masses):

- Line 2654–2655 (K definition): `$\bigl|\sum_{k=1}^{N_c} s_k \sqrt{\hat m_k}\,\bigr|^2$`
- Line 1837, 1840 (derivation): `$|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$` — uses bare $m_k$ = quark masses, correct
- Lines 2719, 3017, 3214, 3265: all use `$\hat m_k$`

The one instance of $M^k{}_k$ in the neighborhood of the Kähler discussion is line 2772:
`$\zch_k = \sqrt{m_k} \sim 1/\!\sqrt{\langle M^k{}_k\rangle}$` — which is explicitly and correctly explaining the map. No problem here.

---

## Issue 2: Precision summary table (around line 382) — internal consistency

The table is mostly consistent, but there are two notable problems:

**2a. $v_0$-doubling precision mismatch (line 3133 vs body text).**
The summary/status table at line 3133 says: "$v_0$-doubling observed to $0.025\%$"
But throughout the body (lines 367, 416, 2818), the precision of the $m_b$ prediction is stated as "$0.07\%$". Both numbers are arithmetically correct but measure different things: $0.025\%$ = deviation of the ratio $v_0^{(f)}/v_0^{(s)}$ from exactly 2 (i.e. $(2.0005-2)/2$), while $0.07\%$ = the resulting $m_b$ prediction error ($|4177-4180|/4180$). The table's $0.025\%$ is the tighter-looking number and is not what the body text uses. This is potentially misleading to a reader and not cross-referenced.

**2b. $m_b$ sigma value: "$0.1\sigma$" vs "$0.06\sigma$"** — used inconsistently:
- Lines 365 and 2768: "to $0.1\sigma$"
- Lines 1878, 2818, and line 3141: "at $0.06\sigma$"

The physical difference: $0.1\sigma$ uses only the PDG uncertainty ($30$ MeV), while $0.06\sigma$ adds the prediction uncertainty ($\pm40$ MeV) in quadrature: $3/\sqrt{30^2+40^2} = 3/50 = 0.06$. Both are used without explanation of the difference. This is an inconsistency that should be resolved: pick one and explain.

**2c. The $\delta \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$ ppm and sigma values are internally self-consistent throughout the paper** (5 ppm, 3.6σ LEE, 4.7σ specific appear at lines 643–652, 2929, 3134). However, the project memory records an older corrected value of "35 ppm, 3.1σ". The paper's 5 ppm is arithmetically: $|{-1.1\times10^{-6}}|/(2/9) \approx 5$ ppm relative to $2/9$. The memory value of "35 ppm" would correspond to a residual of $\sim7.8\times10^{-6}$ relative to $2/9$, or equivalently the residual relative to the full domain $2\pi/3 \approx 2.094$. This discrepancy between the memory notes and the paper text should be investigated — the memory and paper disagree on the fundamental numbers here.

---

## Issue 3: Yukawa terms — composite vs elementary distinction not explained

This is the most significant gap. The unified superpotential at lines 2675–2677 writes:
$$y_c\, H\, M^c{}_c + y_b\, H\, M^b{}_b + y_t\, H\, Q^t \bar Q_t$$

The paper explains the $H Q^t \bar Q_t$ term for the top (elementary quarks, non-confined) at lines 2579–2598, correctly. However, there is a logical problem with the $H M^c{}_c$ and $H M^b{}_b$ terms:

- Lines 2503–2504 state that charm and bottom quarks are **"integrated out at tree level"**, reducing to $N_f = 3$. If $c$ and $b$ are integrated out, the meson superfields $M^c{}_c$ and $M^b{}_b$ do not exist as propagating degrees of freedom in the EFT.
- Yet the unified $W$ (lines 2669–2678) uses $M^c{}_c$ and $M^b{}_b$ in the Yukawa terms, immediately after specifying that $M^{(3)}$ is the $3\times3$ light-flavor block $(u,d,s)$ (line 2680).
- The paper **nowhere explains** whether $y_c H M^c_c$ is: (a) a UV operator in the 5-flavor theory above the threshold, (b) a matching condition at the threshold, or (c) a residual composite operator surviving below threshold. The earlier text at lines 2586–2598 writes the Yukawa in the quark language ($y_{ij} H Q^i \bar Q_j$) for the heavy sector — but then the unified W switches to the meson language ($M^c{}_c$) without bridging the two.

This creates a conceptual inconsistency: the paper "integrates out" $c,b$ to get the $N_f=3$ confined theory, then re-introduces $M^c{}_c$ and $M^b{}_b$ in the Yukawa sector without explanation. The reader is not told whether these are the same $M$ fields, UV remnants, or something else. The sentence at line 2680 only clarifies that $M^{(3)}$ is the $3\times3$ block — which implicitly acknowledges that $M^c{}_c$ and $M^b{}_b$ are from the full 5-flavor $M$ — but then the confinement discussion only applies to the 3-flavor sector.

A one-sentence clarification is missing: e.g., "The terms $y_c H M^c_c$ and $y_b H M^b_b$ are operators in the full 5-flavor theory before threshold matching; after integrating out $c$ and $b$, these reduce to the matching conditions that set $\Lambda_{\text{eff}}$ and generate the Seiberg constraint at scale $m_c, m_b$." Or alternatively, clarify that the Yukawa terms for $c,b$ are written above the confinement scale where $M^c_c = Q^c \cdot \bar Q_c$ makes sense.

---

## Issue 4: Other notation inconsistencies found

**4a. The spurion F-term: inconsistent notation and inconsistent values.**

Multiple incompatible formulations appear:
- Abstract (line 62): `$\langle F\rangle = f_\pi^2$`
- Line 1890: `$\langle F_{\Phi_0}\rangle \sim f_\pi$` (different symbol, different value)
- Line 1953: `$\langle F\rangle \sim (250\text{ MeV})^3/92\text{ MeV} \approx 1.7\times10^5\text{ MeV}^2$` — this is $(230\text{ MeV})^3/f_\pi$, not $f_\pi^2 \approx 8501\text{ MeV}^2$; these are incompatible
- Line 2555: `$\langle F\rangle \sim f_\pi$` (value $\approx 92$ MeV$^2$... or MeV?)
- Line 2694: `$\langle F_S\rangle = f_\pi^2$` (different symbol, for spurion specifically)
- Line 2779: `$\langle F\rangle \sim f_\pi$`
- Line 3218: `$\langle F\rangle = f_\pi^2$`

The physical interpretation varies: the chiral condensate estimate $(250^3/92)$ MeV$^2 \approx 1.7\times10^5$ MeV$^2$ is genuinely different from $f_\pi^2 \approx 8501$ MeV$^2$. The paper mixes up three different F-term scales and three different symbols ($F$, $F_{\Phi_0}$, $F_S$) without a unified notation. The abstract's $\langle F\rangle = f_\pi^2$ disagrees with the body's $\langle F\rangle \sim f_\pi$.

**4b. Table column header "$v_0^2$ (MeV)" dimensionality note (line 387).**
The awkward dimensionality ($v_0$ in MeV$^{1/2}$, so $v_0^2$ in MeV) is only explained parenthetically at line 3169 in the conclusions but not at the table definition. A reader encountering the table first might wonder why $v_0^2$ is in MeV (not MeV$^2$). The table header is technically correct but unintuitive; the parenthetical clarification at 3169 is well after first appearance.

**4c. Lepton Koide precision: "0.001%" in table is inconsistent with "$|Q-3/2|= 1.38\times10^{-5}$" in body.**

Line 392 and 395 say $(e,\mu,\tau)$ has "0.001%" deviation from $2/3$. However the body at line 574 says $|Q-3/2| = 1.38\times10^{-5}$. In the $Q=2/3$ convention, the deviation is $|Q-2/3| = |1.38\times10^{-5}/Q| \approx 1.38\times10^{-5}/(3/2) \cdot (2/3)^2$... actually these measure different things with different $Q$ normalizations. Converting: the $Q=2/3$ convention gives $|Q - 2/3| = |1/(1+1.38\times10^{-5}) \cdot (2/3) - 2/3| \approx (2/3)\times1.38\times10^{-5}\approx 9.2\times10^{-6}$. As a percentage: $9.2\times10^{-6}/(2/3) \approx 1.38\times10^{-5}$ = $0.00138\%$ ≈ $0.001\%$. So the "$0.001\%$" in the table is correct when using the $Q=2/3$ convention. The "$1.38\times10^{-5}$" in the body uses the $Q=3/2$ convention. These are consistent. No problem here, but the dual-convention situation is something a reader needs to track.

**4d. Soft-term A-term notation: $A_y y_i H M^i{}_i$ vs Yukawa definitions.**

Line 2700 writes $A_y y_i H M^i{}_i$ in the soft Lagrangian — but the Yukawa terms in $W$ at line 2675–2677 use different couplings for $c,b,t$ (composite vs elementary). The soft A-term sum over $i$ with $M^i{}_i$ (which presumably includes the top via $M^t{}_t$, which doesn't exist) may be overreaching. There's no clarification that for the top sector, the A-term would be written as $A_y y_t H Q^t \bar Q_t$ instead.
